worth_it_explanation: Amazon DSP ships with permissive defaults. Without an audit, it is straightforward to spend months at scale on made-for-advertising (MFA) sites, low-quality app environments, or content categories a brand cannot be seen beside. The controls exist in the console and take minutes to configure; confirming they are set is a quick check that protects both spend efficiency and brand reputation.
intro: Amazon DSP provides a layered set of brand safety controls: Amazon's built-in inventory quality filters, configurable content category exclusions, and advertiser-managed site and app block lists. These checks confirm that those controls are switched on and tuned rather than left at permissive defaults, that exclusion lists cover the adjacency categories the brand cannot accept, and that the settings are applied consistently across every active line item rather than set once on a template and missed on subsequent work.

audit_checks.title: Amazon DSP brand safety settings are at permissive defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Permissive defaults allow ad delivery into made-for-advertising sites, sensitive content categories, and low-quality app environments. The spend still exits the account and the impressions still count, but they run in placements the brand would not choose knowingly. The financial and reputational exposure scales with budget.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP includes brand safety settings at the advertiser, order, and line item levels. At the advertiser level, 'Amazon Brand Safety' applies Amazon's curated inventory quality filters across all campaigns under that advertiser profile. Content category exclusions let advertisers block specific sensitive content types, such as adult content, violent video games, or politically contentious material. A separate made-for-advertising (MFA) filter targets sites that exist primarily to generate ad revenue rather than serve a real audience.

None of these controls are fully on by default. The platform ships with settings that favor reach, so a new advertiser profile or a line item cloned from an old one may carry no category exclusions and no MFA filtering. Amazon's own brand safety guidance notes that brand safety requires ongoing configuration rather than a one-time setup, which implies the defaults alone may not match a brand's own adjacency standards.

A few signs point to settings being at permissive defaults:
  • The advertiser profile shows no content category exclusions selected
  • The MFA site filter is not enabled at the advertiser or line item level
  • The Amazon Brand Safety setting is off or set to its least restrictive tier
  • Recently created line items do not carry the exclusions that older ones have
You may want to review the settings at the advertiser level first because those flow down, and then spot-check a sample of active line items to confirm the settings applied rather than staying at the defaults.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic brand selling on Amazon, MFA traffic produces impressions but no shopping signal. Amazon's attribution model measures downstream Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) purchases, and MFA placements deliver neither clicks that convert nor audiences that return. Enabling the MFA filter removes inventory that spends budget without contributing to the retail outcomes the campaign is measured on.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser driving to an off-Amazon destination, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel measures post-click conversions. MFA and low-quality app placements inflate impression counts without improving the pixel's conversion signal. Filtering them out leaves cleaner traffic for the pixel to measure and a more accurate picture of which placements are actually working.

learn_more.source_title: A beginner's guide to brand safety
Content adjacency policies for Amazon DSP

investigate.intro: Confirm the Amazon Brand Safety setting, content category exclusions, and MFA filter state at both the advertiser and line item levels.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to 'Advertiser settings' for the advertiser you are auditing.
Locate the brand safety section and note which content category exclusions are selected, whether the Amazon Brand Safety filter is on, and at which tier it is set.
Note whether the MFA site filter is enabled at the advertiser level.

Navigate to 'Orders', then open a sample of active line items, at least 3 to 5, and check whether each line item carries content category exclusions or inherits them. Note any line item where the advertiser-level settings do not appear to have been applied.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the advertiser-level content category exclusions, the Amazon Brand Safety tier, and the MFA filter status. For each sampled line item, the practitioner notes whether those settings are present. A pass requires the Amazon Brand Safety filter to be on, at least the content categories the brand cannot accept to be excluded, and the MFA filter to be enabled. A line item with no exclusions and a permissive Amazon Brand Safety setting is a fail recorded with the line item name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the brand safety setting state and the MFA filter status at the advertiser level, and note the exclusion status for each sampled line item above.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon DSP advertiser profile for {{site_name}} is running with brand safety settings at or near permissive defaults. Content category exclusions are absent or incomplete, the made-for-advertising filter is not enabled, and the Amazon Brand Safety setting is not configured to reflect the brand's standards. Budget is reaching placements the brand would not choose, and the exposure scales with campaign spend. {{voice_recommend}} enabling the Amazon Brand Safety filter, selecting the content category exclusions that match the brand's adjacency standards, and turning on the MFA filter at the advertiser level. After the changes, you may want to spot-check a sample of active line items to confirm the settings applied consistently across the account.

audit_checks.title: Site and app exclusion lists are absent or not applied to active line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Content category filters block broad classes of inventory, but they do not prevent delivery to specific sites or apps that fall within an allowed category but are nonetheless unsuitable for the brand. Exclusion lists are the second layer of protection, and without them the brand's ads run wherever the targeting and bid logic place them.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows advertisers to upload site block lists and app block lists, which prevent delivery to specific domains or app identifiers regardless of whether those placements pass the content category filters. A political news site, for example, may not trigger a 'sensitive content' category exclusion but may still be a placement the brand wants to avoid. Explicit exclusion lists are the way to block those specific placements.

The lists operate at the order or line item level. A list attached to one order does not automatically apply to others. When a campaign structure grows across multiple orders, each order needs the list assigned, and new line items added later need to inherit it. Without a consistent application process, the list protects some line items and not others.

The Amazon DSP inventory policies define what Amazon itself blocks, but those policies are Amazon's floor, not the brand's standard. Advertiser-managed exclusion lists exist to enforce the brand's own standard above that floor.

A few signs point to exclusion list gaps:
  • No site block list or app block list is present under the advertiser's shared assets
  • Active orders do not have an exclusion list assigned
  • The exclusion list was last updated many months ago and has not grown as the account has run
You may want to confirm that a list exists, that it is assigned to each active order, and that the process for updating it when new unsuitable placements are identified is understood.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic brand, placements on competitor product-review sites or deal aggregators may pass the content category filters but still carry reputational risk. An explicit block list is the practical way to prevent delivery to those specific domains without blocking an entire content category that also contains useful inventory.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser, placements on sites with audiences misaligned from the campaign's intent waste impressions. A domain block list built from placement reports, which are available in Amazon DSP reporting, gives the practitioner a data-driven way to identify and remove those placements over time.

learn_more.source_title: A beginner's guide to brand safety

investigate.intro: Confirm whether site and app block lists exist, whether they are assigned to active orders, and whether the list contents reflect the brand's adjacency standards.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: In the Amazon DSP console, navigate to 'Brand safety and inventory' or the shared assets area and check whether a site block list and an app block list are present for this advertiser.
Open a sample of active orders and confirm that each order has an exclusion list assigned. Note any order that does not.

Review the contents of the exclusion list and note when it was last updated. A list that has not changed in many months may not reflect the full range of placements identified as unsuitable during the campaign's run.

Pull a placement-level performance report for the past 30 days and scan for domains or apps with high impressions but no downstream conversion signal. These are candidates for the exclusion list if the brand considers them unsuitable.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a site block list and an app block list exist, how many items each contains, and whether each active order has a list assigned. A pass requires at least one exclusion list present and assigned to all active orders. An account with no exclusion list, or with active orders that have no list assigned, is a fail recorded with the affected orders listed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Confirm whether exclusion lists exist and whether each active order has one assigned, then record the finding above.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP account lacks adequate site and app exclusion lists, or that the lists present are not assigned consistently across active orders. Amazon's content category filters establish a floor, but they do not prevent delivery to specific domains or apps that are unsuitable for the brand but fall within an allowed category. Without exclusion lists, the placement mix is determined entirely by the targeting and bid logic, with no advertiser-level override for known unsuitable placements. {{voice_recommend}} creating or expanding a site block list and an app block list that reflect the brand's adjacency standards, assigning those lists to every active order, and establishing a process for reviewing placement reports and adding new unsuitable domains as the campaigns run.

audit_checks.title: Brand safety settings are not applied consistently across active line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An account where some line items carry exclusions and others do not leaves the brand partially exposed. The protected line items spend into filtered inventory; the unprotected ones do not. The exposure is proportional to the budget running on the unprotected line items, and it is not visible in account-level metrics.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP applies brand safety settings at the advertiser, order, and line item levels. Advertiser-level settings flow down to orders and line items created after they are set, but they do not retroactively update existing line items. A line item created before the advertiser-level settings were configured, or duplicated from an older line item, may carry a different and less restrictive set of controls than newer work built with the settings in place.

The result is an account where brand safety coverage is uneven across the active line item pool. The inconsistency is not visible in summary reporting, which aggregates across all line items. It only surfaces when you open individual line items and compare their settings.

A few signs point to inconsistent application:
  • Older line items show no content category exclusions while newer ones do
  • Line items cloned from an earlier template carry the template's settings rather than the current standard
  • Different orders under the same advertiser show different brand safety configurations with no documented reason for the difference
You may want to treat this as a governance check rather than a content policy check: the brand's standards are presumably consistent, but the configuration history may not be.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic brand, budget split across line items with different safety settings produces a mixed placement pool at the account level. The Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) purchase signal that feeds Amazon's attribution model includes conversions from both the protected and unprotected placements. Reviewing the placement-level performance report and filtering by line item can show whether the unprotected line items are generating clean signal or surface-level impression volume.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel aggregates conversion events across all placements. If some line items are running into MFA or sensitive-content inventory, those placements produce low or zero conversion rates that dilute the account's performance picture. Filtering the placement report by line item makes the split visible.

learn_more.source_title: A beginner's guide to brand safety

investigate.intro: Compare brand safety settings across a representative sample of active line items to identify inconsistencies.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: In the Amazon DSP console, open the advertiser and navigate to the 'Orders' list.
Select at least 5 active line items from different orders, including both older and recently created ones.
Open each line item and record the content category exclusions present, whether an exclusion list is assigned, and the Amazon Brand Safety setting.

Compare the settings across the sampled line items. Note any line item whose settings differ from the others without a documented reason, and note the budget running on each so the exposure from the inconsistency can be estimated.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If the advertiser has access to Amazon Marketing Cloud, you may want to consider running a query that groups performance by line item and breaks down impression volume by domain or app category.
A line item delivering into a higher proportion of low-quality placements relative to others with the same targeting may indicate that its brand safety settings are less restrictive.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the brand safety settings for each sampled line item and notes whether the settings are consistent across the sample. A pass requires all active line items to carry equivalent content category exclusions, an exclusion list assignment, and equivalent Amazon Brand Safety settings. A line item that deviates from the account's intended standard is a fail recorded with the line item name, its budget, and the specific setting that differs.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the brand safety settings for each sampled line item above and note where settings differ across the account.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that brand safety settings are applied inconsistently across {{site_name}}'s active Amazon DSP line items. Some line items carry content category exclusions and exclusion list assignments while others do not, leaving a portion of the account's budget running into placements that the protected line items specifically exclude. The inconsistency is not visible in account-level reporting and only surfaces when individual line item settings are compared. {{voice_recommend}} auditing every active line item's brand safety configuration, bringing the unprotected line items into alignment with the account's intended standard, and establishing a process for confirming settings on any new line item before it goes live.
